title: Why some rooms feel honest
summary: Reflect on why certain physical spaces feel trustworthy or authentic — and what design, light, or use contributes to that quality.
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) describing a room that felt honest or trustworthy to you — a place where nothing was pretending to be something it was not. Describe the room in detail, explain what made it feel that way, and reflect on what 'honesty' in a space might actually mean. Use present simple for general observations, past simple for personal description, at least one concession with 'although' or 'while', and at least one modal verb.
hintStarters: The most honest room I have ever been in was not beautiful in any designed way — it was honest because…
Although the room looked nothing special from the outside, something about the way the light fell made it feel…
Some spaces feel like they are performing — the furniture chosen for effect, the colours calculated to impress — but this room…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thể hiện tuổi tác một cách rõ ràng, không che giấu
lớp bề mặt hình thành qua quá trình sử dụng
gỗ để lộ, không che phủ
cảm giác được sắp xếp có chủ ý
tạo điều kiện cho cuộc trò chuyện
cảm giác bền vững, lâu dài
loại bỏ những thứ không cần thiết, trả về dạng cơ bản
cảm thấy thoải mái, thư giãn
bề mặt chưa hoàn thiện, thô ráp
tự kể câu chuyện của mình
cấu trúc cơ bản của một tòa nhà
không có gì giả vờ là cái gì khác

sampleText:
The most honest room I have ever spent time in was a working kitchen in a farmhouse in the Alentejo region of Portugal. The walls were whitewashed and uneven, the table had been scrubbed so many times that the grain of the wood ran clean and pale, and the shelves held exactly what was used most: dried herbs, a few heavy pans, jars of salt and oil. Nothing was pretending to be decorative. Everything was there because it performed a function, and the marks of all that use — the stains, the chips, the worn corners — were left where they were. The room wore its age openly.

Although many modern kitchens are designed to look effortless and clean, they often feel curated in a way that makes you reluctant to touch anything. This room felt the opposite. You could tell that real cooking happened here because the surfaces had a patina of use that no designer could replicate. The ceiling showed exposed timber, darkened by years of heat and smoke. The light came from one window and fell in a way that made everything look considered — not arranged for effect, but simply honest. While the farmhouse was not wealthy or particularly old, the kitchen had a sense of permanence that expensive renovated spaces rarely achieve. It felt as though it had been in use for a long time and would continue to be for a long time more.

I think honesty in a room might mean that the space does not ask you to admire it. It does not perform. It is simply what it is, and it invites you to be simply what you are too. A room that strips everything back to the bones of a building can sometimes make you feel more at ease than a designed space that has thought about every detail. The farmhouse kitchen told its own story through its objects and its wear. You could read it the way you read a face — the lines and marks not as flaws but as evidence of a life that has actually been lived.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present simple for general reflections
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I think honesty in a room might mean that the space does not ask you to admire it' dùng hiện tại đơn vì đây là quan điểm chung của tác giả, không giới hạn ở thời điểm cụ thể. Việc chuyển từ quá khứ đơn (mô tả phòng) sang hiện tại đơn (suy ngẫm chung) là một kỹ thuật cấu trúc đoạn văn B1 quan trọng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I think honesty in a room might mean that the space does not ask you to admire it' uses present simple because it expresses the writer's general view, not limited to a specific moment. Shifting from past simple (room description) to present simple (general reflection) is a key B1 paragraph structure technique.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession with 'although'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although many modern kitchens are designed to look effortless and clean, they often feel curated' thừa nhận thực tế (nhà bếp hiện đại trông đẹp) trước khi phản bác (chúng cảm thấy giả tạo). Đây là cách xây dựng lập luận điển hình ở cấp B1 — thừa nhận quan điểm đối lập trước rồi mới đưa ra luận điểm chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although many modern kitchens are designed to look effortless and clean, they often feel curated' acknowledges a fact (modern kitchens look nice) before countering it (they feel artificial). This is a typical B1 argument structure — concede the opposing view before making the main point.

sampleTranslationVi:
Căn phòng trung thực nhất tôi từng ở là một nhà bếp đang được sử dụng trong một trang trại ở vùng Alentejo của Bồ Đào Nha. Các bức tường được quét vôi trắng và không đều, chiếc bàn đã được cọ rửa nhiều lần đến mức vân gỗ trở nên sạch và nhạt màu, và các kệ chứa đúng những gì được dùng nhiều nhất: thảo mộc khô, vài chiếc chảo nặng, lọ muối và dầu. Không có gì giả vờ là đồ trang trí. Mọi thứ ở đó đều thực hiện một chức năng, và những dấu vết của việc sử dụng — những vết bẩn, những mảnh vỡ, những góc bị mòn — được giữ nguyên ở đó. Căn phòng thể hiện tuổi tác của nó một cách rõ ràng.

Mặc dù nhiều nhà bếp hiện đại được thiết kế trông có vẻ dễ dàng và sạch sẽ, chúng thường cảm thấy được sắp xếp có chủ ý đến mức bạn không muốn chạm vào bất cứ thứ gì. Căn phòng này cảm thấy ngược lại. Bạn có thể biết rằng việc nấu ăn thực sự diễn ra ở đây vì các bề mặt có lớp bề mặt từ quá trình sử dụng mà không nhà thiết kế nào có thể tái tạo. Trần nhà hiện ra những thanh gỗ để lộ, bị tối màu bởi nhiều năm nhiệt và khói. Ánh sáng đến từ một cửa sổ và chiếu xuống theo cách khiến mọi thứ trông có chủ ý — không sắp xếp để tạo hiệu ứng, mà đơn giản là thành thật. Trong khi trang trại không giàu có hay đặc biệt cũ, nhà bếp có cảm giác bền vững mà những không gian được cải tạo đắt tiền hiếm khi đạt được. Nó cảm giác như đã được sử dụng trong một thời gian dài và sẽ tiếp tục được dùng trong một thời gian dài hơn nữa.

Tôi nghĩ sự thành thật trong một căn phòng có thể có nghĩa là không gian đó không yêu cầu bạn phải ngưỡng mộ nó. Nó không biểu diễn. Nó đơn giản là chính nó, và nó mời bạn cũng đơn giản là chính mình. Một căn phòng loại bỏ mọi thứ đến tận cấu trúc cơ bản của tòa nhà đôi khi có thể khiến bạn cảm thấy thoải mái hơn so với một không gian được thiết kế tỉ mỉ từng chi tiết. Nhà bếp trang trại tự kể câu chuyện của mình qua đồ vật và dấu vết sử dụng. Bạn có thể đọc nó như cách bạn đọc một khuôn mặt — những nếp nhăn và vết tích không phải là khiếm khuyết mà là bằng chứng của một cuộc sống đã thực sự được sống.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'nothing was + gerund' to describe an absence of pretence?
options: Nothing pretended to be decorative.
Nothing was pretending to be decorative.
Nothing was pretend to be decorative.
Nothing has been pretending to be decorative.
mcQuestions.explanation: In a past narrative, past simple 'Nothing pretended to be decorative' is most natural. 'Nothing was pretending' (past continuous) works too but emphasises ongoing action. In the sample text the past continuous is used deliberately. Both options A and C are grammatically sound; C is the cleaner choice in a standard narrative.
mcQuestions.hint: Past simple is usually sufficient for one-off descriptions. Past continuous emphasises an ongoing state.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to introduce a concession?
options: Although many modern kitchens look effortless, but they often feel artificial.
Although many modern kitchens look effortless, they often feel artificial.
Although, many modern kitchens look effortless, they often feel artificial.
Many modern kitchens look effortless, although because they often feel artificial.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no comma after 'although', no 'but' before the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: No comma after 'although'; no 'but' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'might mean' correctly to express possibility in a general reflection?
options: Honesty in a room might means that the space does not ask you to admire it.
Honesty in a room might to mean that the space does not ask you to admire it.
Honesty in a room might mean that the space does not ask you to admire it.
Honesty in a room might meaning that the space does not ask you to admire it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal 'might' is always followed by the bare infinitive 'mean', not 'means', 'to mean', or 'meaning'.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal verbs are always followed by the bare infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'while' to contrast two ideas?
options: While the farmhouse was not wealthy, but the kitchen had a sense of permanence.
While, the farmhouse was not wealthy, the kitchen had a sense of permanence.
The farmhouse was not wealthy while the kitchen also had a sense of permanence.
While the farmhouse was not wealthy, the kitchen had a sense of permanence.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'While + clause, main clause' — no comma after 'while', no extra conjunction.
mcQuestions.hint: No comma after 'while'; no 'but' before the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'could + bare infinitive' for past possibility?
options: You could tell that real cooking happened here.
You could told that real cooking happened here.
You could to tell that real cooking happened here.
You could telling that real cooking happened here.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could + bare infinitive' — no 'to', no past tense form, no gerund after 'could'.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal verbs are always followed by the bare infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'rarely + achieve' correctly for a general truth?
options: Expensive renovated spaces rarely achieves a sense of permanence.
Expensive renovated spaces rarely achieve a sense of permanence.
Expensive renovated spaces are rarely achieving a sense of permanence.
Expensive renovated spaces rarely achieved a sense of permanence.
mcQuestions.explanation: Plural subject 'spaces' takes 'achieve' (no -s). 'Rarely' is a frequency adverb that goes before the main verb in present simple.
mcQuestions.hint: Plural subject + frequency adverb before main verb: 'spaces rarely achieve'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'make + object + feel' as a causative?
options: A well-designed room can make you to feel at ease.
A well-designed room can make you feeling at ease.
A well-designed room can make you feel at ease.
A well-designed room can makes you feel at ease.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make + object + bare infinitive' — no 'to', no gerund after the object. After 'can', the bare infinitive 'make' is used.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Make + object + bare infinitive': no 'to' between the object and the following verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'tell its own story' to describe a room communicating meaning?
options: The kitchen told its own story by its objects and its wear.
The kitchen told its own story with of its objects and its wear.
The kitchen told its own story from its objects and its wear.
The kitchen told its own story through its objects and its wear.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tell a story through' = the medium or means used to communicate. 'Through' is the correct preposition here.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Tell a story through' = by means of. 'Through' not 'by', 'with', or 'from'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'feel like + noun phrase' to make a comparison?
options: The room felt as though it had been in use for a long time.
The room felt as it had been in use for a long time.
The room felt as though it has been in use for a long time.
The room felt like it has been in use for a long time.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Feel as though + clause' uses past perfect ('had been') in the clause when describing a past impression. 'As though' requires a full clause; 'feel like' in informal use also works but 'as though' is more formal.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Feel as though + clause' — use past perfect in the clause for a past impression.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'not … but …' correctly to contrast two descriptions?
options: The light fell in a way that looked considered — not arranged for effect, but simply honesty.
The light fell in a way that looked considered — not arranged for effect, but simply honest.
The light fell in a way that looked considered — not arranged for effect, but honest simply.
The light fell in a way that looked considered — not arrangement for effect, but simply honest.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not X, but Y' requires parallel structure. 'Arranged for effect' (past participle phrase) is contrasted with 'simply honest' (adverb + adjective) — both modify the light's quality.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Not X, but Y' needs parallel structure on both sides.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'evidence of' + noun clause?
options: The marks were evidence for a life that had actually been lived.
The marks were evidence about a life that had actually been lived.
The marks were evidence of a life that had actually been lived.
The marks were evidence a life that had actually been lived.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Evidence of + noun/clause' — the correct preposition is 'of'. 'Evidence for' means 'proof supporting a claim', which changes the meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Evidence of' = something that shows or demonstrates. Always 'of'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Can honesty be designed into a space, or does it only come from use and time? Is a brand-new building that looks old — exposed brick, rough plaster — honest, or is it just another form of performance?
